spec_match.rationale: The migration is fundamentally about reliability, not throughput — the chromestatus summary explicitly says the web API and semantics don't change. A bulk-write throughput benchmark is a reasonable proxy but does not target the actual SQLite-vs-LevelDB difference the feature is shipping.

interactive.rationale: Real controls (object count + size inputs, run + wipe buttons) drive an IndexedDB workload and update four numeric stat tiles live.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises a workload that inserts and reads back at scale and reports throughput; the page delivers that exactly.

edge_cases.rationale: Happy path only. No abort/rollback workload, no very-large-object case (>50MB triggers different code paths), no constraint-violation, no quota-exceeded behaviour — all of which are where the SQLite backend changes matter.

design_system.rationale: Variables-only styling, the rose Wipe DB button overrides background AND color, scoreboard uses real `.stat` cards (not the disallowed static-card pattern).

openQuestions.title: Detect and label the active IndexedDB backend
openQuestions.detail: Chrome 149 ships step 2 of a staged rollout — incognito uses SQLite already (step 1), new persistent stores get it now (step 2), existing stores migrate later (step 3). The page should probe whether the store it just created is SQLite-backed (e.g. via the Storage Buckets API metadata, or a chrome://flags hint) and display the backend name. Without this the numbers can't be compared meaningfully across Chrome versions.

openQuestions.title: Add a baseline-comparison view across runs
openQuestions.detail: The page reports one set of throughput numbers per run. Persist the last N runs in a small table (or localStorage) so a user can see drift between cold and warm, between Chrome versions, or between Incognito and normal mode — which is the only way the perf claim becomes evidence rather than vibes.

openQuestions.title: Surface aborted-transaction recovery as part of the workload
openQuestions.detail: The SQLite migration's headline reliability improvement is around abort/rollback consistency. Add an `abort midway` toggle that mid-transaction calls `transaction.abort()` and verifies the store is restored to the pre-transaction state. This puts the demo on the actual diff between backends.

openQuestions.title: Test concurrent writer + reader scenario
openQuestions.detail: The LevelDB backend had a long tail of bugs around concurrent transactions. Add a parallel pair of transactions (one writing, one reading) that asserts isolation. This is also what the sibling `concurrent-reads-test` does, but a bulk-write stress page is the natural place to demonstrate write/read interleaving.

summary: Functionally solid as a throughput probe, but it benchmarks performance for a feature that explicitly does not promise faster performance. The big missing piece is a backend-detection probe so the numbers can be tied to which storage engine is actually running, plus an abort/rollback scenario that targets the reliability improvements the SQLite migration is actually shipping.
